speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about now when you-- this experience, that was happening to you at Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Breslau?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You were telling me that the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Russians were approaching.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we weren't there already. We were marching away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how that all happened, what happened that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was, in the morning, um, I could-- we going-- we-- we were supposed to go to work, and we didn't know why. Why did they send us to work on Sunday? We never went to work. And an hour later, they brought us back. And um, and they said that we're going to march and very far away. Uh, and that's it. And we- and right away we started to go up from Breslau because the Russians were so close. They were so nervous, those-- those Gestapos, they were terrible. They were like, crazy. And we started to march.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were also-- we had like a little [INAUDIBLE], you know? When people get sick-- because we were workers. So they had that. They said they had a nurse. I don't know, I never saw a doctor there. I also had my ear-- I had a boil in my ear, and the nurse said-- uh, I mean, she opened it there and she [INAUDIBLE] from my ear. She, uh-- I can hear well-- I could hear well right away. I mean, you know, but I have this ear was good. Well anyway, it's now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was people and [INAUDIBLE] was sick. And they put him in a wagon and we had to-- we had to pull him with us. As we were walking, we had a big wagon with people who were in the wagon, and um, they were hitting us, left, right. They were screaming, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's fast, so fast, walking fast. And-- and we were the people-- I was so cold. It was absolutely terrible. And people were just, uh, dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you walking in any specific formation and did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were walking out in the forest, in the forest. They didn't-- first when we went out from Breslau, yeah. But after, they took us through the forest. They, uh- you know, nobody should see us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of shoes and clothing did you have on at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got-- when I was in Breslau I got an overall. It was like pants, but the whole thing is one piece. That I had, and we had wooden shoes, no socks, nothing. And we were marching three nights and three days. Half the people, from the girls, died. It was impossible. It was-- we had to be a little aggressive. I always managed somehow, from somewhere, a little bit of food. I didn't tell you, even in Auschwitz, there were women who were working there and they came in and they selecting. And I had somebody I know well. She used to bring me a little package. You know, people who brought some food -- they didn't know where they're going. So I always managed to have a little bit, even in Breslau. The foreman liked me a lot. I was a good worker. He put on sometimes a piece of bread there. It's a little sandwich, he says and he showed me, there it is. He wasn't allowed to do that. And that meant a lot to me and my sister. And then, in Breslau, I forgot to tell you, that I had a friend also, and she was sewing for the SS woman and I helped her out a little bit. And she gave me also, not at the time when I was working, when I was marching, let's say, uh, uh, uh, the other day I had a little bit of time and she got a plate of s-- a big plate of food. And that's what she gave me also. You know, that meant a lot to survive, because it was awful. You couldn't survive if you didn't have a little bit from here or there, you know, a little bit of food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So even when we we're marching, six days and six nights. In the first three days, one German said that he's going to cook for us, potatoes. You know, he had those animals there, the cows with the pigs, things like that. And that thing he cooked for us, those potatoes. And he made place for us. He has a big place, he made place for us to sleep on the floor, you know. So, um, uh- but potatoes, who wants to bring the potatoes? But he had to bring it because it was somewhere else where they were cooked. So they pick. So I was running to help out, you know, meanwhile I was stuffing myself with potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I was so hot, I was burning my, my-- yeah, and fell down, you know, and because the overalls were closed so it fell down at my feet. And here-- and I had-- and I ate there. And I ate three potatoes they gave or something, so I had a lot more than anybody else, because I had potatoes here and I had potatoes there. And also my sister, my sister was-- if she wouldn't be with me, she wouldn't be alive. That's for sure. So I actually took it for her and for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- and the other- and at night, that was when we came, at night, we had those potatoes. After that, we decided we were always five people together, three sisters and me and my sister. We tried to be together. So we saw-- there was-- we saw that in the back, there was a little window there, and there was a lot- a light up there, you know, where you could go down there and go down in the village. It was a very small village. So we decided to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We climbed down there. They were in the front watching. In the back we didn't have-- nobody watched in the back. They didn't think nobody-- that nobody-- somebody's going to go out or something like that. They were in the front watching out, the SSA. And we went in to that little village. And we said that we're German [GERMAN]. You know, the Germans they were- we were running also away from places where the Russians came. She says, yeah, and you're [GERMAN], ah. She started to give us food to eat and she stuffed a bag with food for me and for her. Well, what happened, it got very dark, and we didn't know where to go back. Went every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they was a little station there. And uh-- and we were hiding in the back, on that place, we were hiding in the back because we didn't know how to go back. And uh, and we figured, in the morning, we'll go back when it's going to be light. It was like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all night we were there. Nobody saw us. If they saw us, they'd probably shoot us or something. I don't know what they did to us. And uh, we came back and it wasn't far from our place, where the rest was there. And they were standing already to go away. They were all lined up to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of my sister. And she had two sisters. I would never go back if not my sister. She was crying all night. She didn't know what happened. And so we came there. The SS looks and asks- right away he says, where have you been? And he looks at that bag and sees it's food. So he hit us in the back, you know, with his rifle. And he told us to go stand there, and we were marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had already food, you know? A little bit of food, and that helps us a lot, the other three days, to march. And what we had, that wagon-- the people-- um, when we came, the first thing, in the forest, they shoot them, all of them, and left them there. That's it. Where did the death march end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were three-- we were six days marching, so uh, I don't know. It was winter. It must have been January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did it end? Where was your destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, uh, Gross Rosen. [INAUDIBLE]. First thing, we came into Gross Rosen, that was also a big shock for us. We saw a huge place and people were hanging there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They didn't have-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] In Gross Rosen they didn't-- they didn't have gas, I think. I'm not sure, but what I think, they just had the crematorium. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And they were hanging the people who died. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They just put them-- they just burned them and that's it, in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- can we stop for a moment, please? I'm sorry, we were interrupted. You were telling me about what you saw in Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Gross Rosen was, again, was a death camp. On the other side, there was also men who were working, but they weren't close to us. Sometimes they threw a little bit food from the other side, too. You know? But we weren't allowed to go there. We were altogether separated. It was a death camp like any other camp. You didn't-- I mean, we didn't work. We just-- most people-- skeletons. I also saw my father there. I saw him. I didn't speak to him. It was like I told you, on the other side was men. They came marching there and it was a cousin, second cousin, actually-- he's alive. He told me that your father is in the back there, and I saw him, but he was like a skeleton. It was awful. All the men were skeletons. He also had wagons with full of dead, but they're pulling-- I saw him when we were marching they told us to go in, in the forest, we shouldn't see because men were marching also through, and they were just skeletons marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if he saw me. You weren't allowed to go there. They hit me because I went close there. It was separated from the women. I mean, it's like a big, iron door there. You know, a huge iron door, and they were on the other side. And you weren't allowed to go to that door but I went. They hit me. I was running back. And I saw him but I don't know if he saw me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the cousin that told you your father was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traubach. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Janko Traubach. He's alive. He's in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Gross Rosen for?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. Maybe four weeks, not long. Then they took us to Mauthausen. Because again, the Russians were coming there. I remember they burned all the papers there, in Gross Rosen. I went out from that place. So they put us on, again, on those cattles, and they put 120 instead of 60. Nobody had a place to stand. There were people fighting around there, inside in the cattle. No food. No nothing. It was just awful. And the people-- we were-- if somebody died, just throw them out. The cattle's open, throw them out. And we had already a place.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were standing on each other feet. That's how crowded it was. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because not just we marched out from Gross Rosen, there was other people also. Those people, they were with me at work, you know? It was other people also. And they just stuffed them in. And we had to-- We're going a little bit and then we stopped because the bombs were coming down. The Germans were hiding. I was going to Mauthausen. And what can I tell you? We were begging the bomb should come to us already, because we couldn't take it anymore. There was no food. They opened that once, twice, the cattles, so we should go out, you know. And uh, it was the toilet or something like that, it was an open field. And uh, so, um, what can I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Mauthausen. It took us six days also to come to Mauthausen because the bombs were coming from everywhere. And it was- the things were broken. The train couldn't go. It was the-- I don't know how to say. Well, anyway, we came to Mauthausen finally. In Mauthausen, they didn't have women. We were the only women there. But still in this-- so we, uh-- they had to change clothes for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They cut my hair again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did they give you in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men's clothing. I had pants and a-- a jacket and a hat, a round hat. And we weren't long there because they didn't have a place for us. So they shipped us to-- we didn't march. We, uh- shipped us with-- again, with those cattles, shipped up to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you actually in Mauthausen for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. I don't know. Two weeks or something like that. At Mauthausen, they didn't have-- what to do with us there, because there were just men. And there was no mixing with men. You know, woman, man wasn't mixing. So, uh, and they didn't have a place for us. That's it. They were shipping one place to the other. I don't know when Mauthausen liberated, but they took us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Bergen-Belsen different than the other camps you had been to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen, was thousands and thousands of people there. We sleep on the floor. You didn't have a place for yourself. Whatever bed you lie down that's where you'll-- in the morning, you wake up, dead people. I don't know how many. They just lie down and they didn't get up. People would die there. They were sick. They had typhus. My sister was very sick. She had tuberculosis. I mean, I find out later that she had tuberculosis. [SIGHS] It was an awful thing. One day, they just didn't have place for us. And they took, I don't know how many girls, and they took us to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time had passed that you were in Bergen-Belsen before they took you to the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well not long. I think that Bergen-Belsen was liberated-- England? England came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Bergen-Belsen for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I don't know, another three weeks, I guess. Uh, after that, they just didn't have anywhere to put the people. There was no place for them. And just to shoot them, and that's it. They took us to the forest. We're marching from the morning, from about 3 o'clock to the forest. We came there, there was wood all over, cut up woods. They say we should put it-- this is a mess here, they said. We have to put it on the side. It should be clean. Who could pick up even-- I don't know how many girls can pick up a piece of wood because everybody was half dead. And uh, after that, I don't know, they said, OK that's it. That was in the afternoon, later in the afternoon. He says, we're going to march again. Everybody should stay in the row there. So I decided not to go back with my sister, with my other three-- other three friends, but we were always together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Gitl, she had my name. One was Breindel and one was Judy, Judith, Judi, Judith. We were always together. And we decided-- actually I was the one who said not to go back. They were afraid. And I said, what's going to happen to us here? What do you think? They're going to kill us anyway. And we didn't just go to stand in a row. We went, they didn't see us. They didn't watch us. They march already, I guess. I don't know. No, they didn't count the people. They didn't count. I mean, they counted maybe when everybody's together. But we were hiding further in the forest. And I guess they got killed. They shoot them. We hear shooting but I don't know if they shoot them or it came from somewhere else. But we were hiding until the morning, it was in the forest. And in the morning, we started-- oh yeah, as soon as we came up in the forest, there's shooting again. So they were running at this side, I was on this side of my sister. And when the shooting stopped, I never saw them again. They didn't make it anymore. Me and my sister were left. We started to march again. It was again to the forest, you know. And finally, I don't know, we hit a little village.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, it was just you and your sister now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just me and my sister. We hit a little village and from the back, it wasn't already deep in the forest. It wasn't like, when you want to go in the village. It was still aside there. And I hear that kids speak Czechoslovakian. And I said to my sister-- and my sister, I was just carrying her. She couldn't walk. I said to my sister, listen, I'm going there, we're going there. She says no, she's not going in. She's afraid. Gonna kill us. I said, if they're coming, we're going to get killed any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in myself. She was staying outside there. And I came in, and she asked me-- yeah. And I said, listen, they took me, in Czechoslovakian, he took me to work and I just didn't go back to that place. I got lost. And I haven't got where to go. She looked at me and she said, like that, [INAUDIBLE] and she gave me a piece of bread and she gave me a glass of milk. Sit down and eat slowly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I finished she says, you could stay here if you want. I says, I would love to but I have my sister outside. There was an older woman also there in her house. It was a neighbor. And uh, she said, I'll take your sister. And that's how we remained there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the women that helped you? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] You know what? I forgot. I forgot her name. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I know her name, first name was Veruschka, Czech name. But the other name, I forgot. Probably going to remind myself. I don't-- I don't know. And then there was that older woman there who took care of my sister. My sister was very sick there. One day she told me, you know, yeah, my sister had a big stomach. She had a big stomach, and uh, and she thought first that she's pregnant. She didn't see it right away. She didn't say right away nothing. But after that, she told me that she can't keep my sister because she has a big stomach and she thinks she's pregnant. I said, no way. My sister is not pregnant. My sister is very sick. She had a full stomach of water. And one day she told me that she can't keep her anymore, because she's sick. She was up nights with her, she says. And she was alone, didn't have a husband. And she doesn't think she could keep her.

speaker: interviewer
text: I start to cry. I says, listen, if she have to go, I have to go. But where? We haven't got a home. We don't know where to go. She says, well, I don't know. She needs a doctor. And somehow, I don't know, she said she has a son, a doctor, but he lives in Prague and that's very far. But she'll see that my sister should get a doctor because she saw I'm crying, and she got pity on us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And um, and they brought a doctor. And they give an injection, and he said she has tuberculosis, very bad one, very sick. And he give us some pills. She got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the village or area that you were in? What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Postrekov. And that's how we were staying til- til, uh- May the 5th, we were liberated. Those people had three Russian soldiers hiding on top-- you know, on top of the house. Like we had-- most Europe, they have that. They had three soldiers hidden there. One day--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're-- I'm sorry, we're going to stop now and go to the next tape. We're running out of tape.
